title: Chris Trevas
content:
{{Top|rwp|ffa}}
{{Update|[[Star Wars: Millennium Falcon: A 3-D Owner's Guide]], [[Star Wars: Darth Vader: A 3-D Reconstruction Log|Darth Vader: A 3-D Surgical Log]]}}
{{Person
|image=[[File:Chris_Trevas.jpg]]
|name=Chris Trevas
|birth=c. [[1974]]<ref name="MySpace">{{WebCite|url=http://profile.myspace.com/index.cfm?fuseaction=user.viewprofile&friendid=79016206|text=Chris Trevas's MySpace|nobackup=1}}</ref>
|death=
|listedpronouns=<!--Only include pronouns if they have been explicitly specified by the individual to which this article pertains.-->
|nationality=[[Wikipedia:United States|American]]<ref name="MySpace" />
|occupation=Illustrator<ref name="Star Wars Artist Series">{{SWArchive|url=eu/explore/profile/f20041209/index.html|text=''Star Wars'' Artist Series: Chris Trevas}}</ref>
|companies=[[Del Rey]], [[Dorling Kindersley]], [[IDG Entertainment]], [[Random House]], [[Scholastic Inc.|Scholastic]], [[Titan Magazines]], [[The Topps Company, Inc.|Topps]], [[West End Games]], [[Wizards of the Coast]]<ref name="Star Wars Artist Series" />
|website=[http://www.christrevas.com christrevas.com]
}}
{{Quote|I consider myself extremely fortunate to be living that childhood dream today making my own mark in the Star Wars universe.|Chris Trevas|<ref name="comcast">{{WebCite|url=http://mywebpages.comcast.net/ctrevas/aboutme.html|text=Trevas Illustration - About me|archivedate=20041216141113}}</ref>}}
'''Christopher J. Trevas''' has been an official ''[[Star Wars]]'' artist since [[1995]]. He began as an illustrator for [[West End Games]]'s ''[[Star Wars: The Roleplaying Game]]'', producing artwork for publications such as ''[[Heroes & Rogues]]'', the ''[[Shadows of the Empire Sourcebook]]'', ''[[Instant Adventures]]'', and the ''[[Star Wars Adventure Journal]]''. He has contributed to numerous other projects, including [[Wizards of the Coast]]'s ''[[Arms & Equipment Guide]]'' and their ''[[Star Wars Miniatures]]'' line, for which he designed many of the figures as well as the packaging art.

Some of Trevas's other works include ''[[Priority: X]]'' from [[Star Wars Galaxy Magazine 12|''Star Wars Galaxy Magazine'' 12]], the "[[Guide to the Grand Army of the Republic]]," and ''[[A Two-Edged Sword]]'', as well as two children's books: ''[[Meltdown on Hoth]]'' and ''[[Anakin to the Rescue]]''. He was the author of the recurring feature in ''[[Star Wars Insider]]'' magazine titled "[[Set Piece]]," which ran from [[Star Wars Insider 54|issue 54]] to [[Star Wars Insider 100|issue 100]]. More recently, Trevas co-illustrated ''[[The New Essential Guide to Alien Species]]'' with [[William O'Connor]], [[Jedi vs. Sith: The Essential Guide to the Force (real-life book)|''Jedi vs. Sith: The Essential Guide to the Force'']] with [[Tommy Lee Edwards]], and ''[[Star Wars Blueprints: The Ultimate Collection]]''. He has also illustrated the ''[[Legacy Era Campaign Guide]]'' by [[Wizards of the Coast]] and ''[[The Essential Atlas]]'', where he cameos as a Human in a game of [[Holochess/Legends|holochess]].

==Biography==
===Early life===
Chris Trevas was three years old when he first saw [[Star Wars: Episode IV A New Hope|''Star Wars'']] in [[1977]], but claims that he has more memories of playing with the toys than of the films themselves.<ref name="Star Wars Artist Series" />

===A career in ''Star Wars''===
[[File:HaveFunStorminDaCastle.png|thumb|left|250px|An early Trevas drawing from ''[[Heroes & Rogues]]'']]
In [[1994]], when [[Lucasfilm]] began recruiting artists for [[Star Wars: Episode I The Phantom Menace|Episode I's]] art department, Trevas submitted a portfolio including various alien sketches, but he was not hired at that time. However, at a science-fiction convention in [[1995]] in [[Wikipedia:Chicago|Chicago]], [[Wikipedia:Illinois|Illinois]], he shared his portfolio with [[West End Games]], who at the time held the ''Star Wars'' role-playing license, and was hired by them a few months later.<ref name="Star Wars Artist Series" /> Trevas produced interior artwork for numerous [[roleplaying game]] (RPG) books for them, including ''[[Heroes & Rogues]]'', the ''[[Star Wars Adventure Journal]]'', and supplements for ''[[The DarkStryder Campaign]]''.

In [[1996]], Trevas graduated from college with a [[Wikipedia:Bachelor of Fine Arts|Bachelor of Fine Arts]] degree in Illustration.<ref name="Star Wars Artist Series" /> He then became a full-time freelance artist,<ref name="christrevas.com">{{WebCite|url=https://christrevas.com|text=Trevas Illustration|archivedate=20200727133147}}</ref> continuing to create interior art for West End's ''[[Shadows of the Empire Sourcebook]]'', ''[[Shadows of the Empire Planets Guide]]'', ''[[Instant Adventures]]'', ''[[Star Wars Trilogy Sourcebook, Special Edition]]'', as well as cover art for ''[[Wretched Hives of Scum & Villainy]]'', ''[[The Black Sands of Socorro]]'' and ''[[Cracken's Threat Dossier]]''. In [[1997]], the year before West End Games' collapse, Trevas began doing work for other ''Star Wars'' licensees, including ''[[Priority: X]]'' for [[The Topps Company, Inc.|Topps]]' [[Star Wars Galaxy Magazine|''Star Wars Galaxy'']] magazine and became a regular artist for [[Scholastic Inc.|Scholastic's]] [[Star Wars Kids (1997)|''Star Wars Kids'']] magazine. He continued to branch out to other companies, illustrating ''[[Meltdown on Hoth]]'' for [[Golden Books]] and "[[The Unseen Planets of Star Wars]]" for ''[[Star Wars Insider]]''.

In [[1998]], Chris Trevas and [[Chris Reiff]] created an unofficial fan site called "The Parts of Star Wars," a guide explaining how many of the props in the ''Star Wars'' films were made and how they could be recreated.<ref name="The Parts of Star Wars">{{WebCite|url=http://www.partsofsw.com|text=The Parts of Star Wars|archivedate=20200807011606}}</ref> The next year, he illustrated [[Wikipedia:Dig (magazine)|''Dig'']] magazine's ''[[The Lost City of Tatooine]]'' and the [[Random House]] children's book ''[[Anakin to the Rescue]]''. In [[2001]], ''Star Wars Insider'' called upon the expertise of Trevas and Reiff for a new section called "[[Set Piece]]," a recurring feature—beginning in [[Star Wars Insider 54|issue 54]]—which takes a shot from one of the films and points out behind-the-scenes facts about props and sets; it also features line art by Trevas of selected props. He also illustrated "[[Unseen Planets of Episode I]]" in [[Star Wars Insider 56|issue 56]], a follow-up to the 1998 [[Original trilogy|classic trilogy]] article.

[[File:S5blaster.jpg|thumb|right|250px|The [[S-5 heavy blaster pistol/Legends|S-5 heavy blaster pistol]] from the ''[[Arms & Equipment Guide]]'', an example of Trevas's line art]]
For ''Star Wars'' 25th anniversary in [[2002]], Trevas wrote and illustrated "Star Wars Prop Art: Creating a Believable Universe" for the [[Celebration II]] souvenir book and created ''Star Wars'' art for [[Wikipedia:Comic-Con|Comic-Con's]] souvenir book. The same year, he began working for [[Wizards of the Coast]], illustrating numerous weapons and technologies for their ''[[Arms & Equipment Guide]]''. In [[2003]], he created the [[Jedi Temple/Legends|Jedi Temple]] computer screens that would appear in ''[[Inside the Worlds of Star Wars: Attack of the Clones]]'', published by [[Dorling Kindersley]]. In the following years, he would do more work for Wizards of the Coast: illustrations for ''[[Ultimate Adversaries]]'' and the [[Star Wars Miniatures#Ultimate Missions|''Ultimate Missions'']] series, for the latter of which he would design the accompanying figurines. Trevas was also one of the many artists who began drawing sketch cards for Topps, after a recommendation by his friend and fellow artist [[Joe Corroney]].<ref name="Star Wars Artist Series" />

In addition to his regular "Set Piece" feature, Trevas illustrated three articles for ''Insider'' in 2005: "[[The Unseen Planets of Episode II]]," "[[Guide to the Grand Army of the Republic]]," and the short story ''[[A Two-Edged Sword]]''. For the story, which featured [[Lightsaber/Legends|lightsaber]]-wielding [[Stormtrooper/Legends|stormtroopers]], Trevas created a physical lightsaber prop to use as reference.<ref name="Traviss & Trevas: Behind the Two-Edged Sword">{{Hyperspace|url=hyperspace/member/insideronline/85/index.html|text=Star Wars Insider #85 Online Supplement: Traviss & Trevas: Behind the Two-Edged Sword}}</ref> He also created a work for ''[[Vader: The Ultimate Guide]]'' which shows [[Anakin Skywalker/Legends|Darth Vader]] ordering the execution of [[Owen Lars/Legends|Owen]] and [[Beru Whitesun Lars/Legends|Beru Lars]]. For [[Celebration III]] that year, he created a piece called "The Circle is Now Complete." Trevas continued to contribute to the Wizards of the Coast RPG with either artwork or design sketches for their [[Champions of the Force (Star Wars Miniatures)|Champions of the Force]], [[Bounty Hunters (Star Wars Miniatures)|Bounty Hunters]], and ''[[Starship Battles]] Miniatures'' series.

[[File:His_visions_returns_Celebration_VI_art.jpg|thumb|left|200px|Trevas's commissioned print for [[Celebration VI]]]]
In 2006, Trevas received from [[Del Rey]] his biggest ''Star Wars'' project yet: ''[[The New Essential Guide to Alien Species]]''. Trevas created fifty-three species images—half of the images in the book; the other half were done by [[William O'Connor]]. Trevas's illustrations included the [[Acklay/Legends|acklay]], [[Bantha/Legends|bantha]], [[Dewback/Legends|dewback]], [[Human/Legends|Human]] (based on his own likeness<ref name="TFN Lit">{{JCFCite|url=posts/25573485|board=Literature|thread=The New Essential Guide to Alien Species|user=Chris_Trevas|date=2006-11-13|time=5:08 PM|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20200801193903/https://boards.theforce.net/threads/the-new-essential-guide-to-alien-species.23156129/page-15}}</ref>), [[Kaleesh/Legends|Kaleesh]], [[Rancor/Legends|rancor]], [[Rodian/Legends|Rodian]], [[Tauntaun/Legends|tauntaun]], [[Twi'lek/Legends|Twi'lek]], and many others. Several of his aliens in the book were intended to be as-of-yet unnamed characters from his previous works.<ref name="TFN Lit" /> For the 30th anniversary of ''Star Wars'' in [[2007]], Trevas was one of almost fifty artists to create a work for [[Celebration IV]].<ref name="More Artists">{{SWArchive|url=community/event/celebration/news20070417.html|text=More Artists at Celebration IV}}</ref> His piece, titled "Star Wars: 30 Years of the Force," celebrated ''A New Hope'' and was obviously inspired by an early promotional piece by [[Ralph McQuarrie]]. Prints of the work were sold in limited numbers at Celebration IV and [[Celebration Europe (2007)|Celebration Europe]], both of which Trevas attended.<ref name="More Artists" /><ref name="Celebration Europe">{{SWArchive|url=community/event/celebration/news20070626b.html|text=Celebration Europe Art: Showcasing Stellar Talent}}</ref>

Trevas's most recent work is [[Jedi vs. Sith: The Essential Guide to the Force (real-life book)|''Jedi vs. Sith: The Essential Guide to the Force'']]. The book, written by [[Ryder Windham]], is illustrated by Trevas and [[Tommy Lee Edwards]]. The two evenly split the workload of 120 illustrations. Trevas, who came onto the project in [[January]] 2007, created sixty pieces for the book, with pieces including the [[funeral of Mara Jade Skywalker]], the [[Jedi Praxeum|Jedi Academy]] confronting the spirit of [[Exar Kun/Legends|Exar Kun]], [[Shmi Skywalker Lars/Legends|Shmi]] and [[Anakin Skywalker/Legends|Anakin Skywalker]] arriving on [[Tatooine/Legends|Tatooine]], [[Jedi historian]] [[Tionne Solusar]], a young [[Palpatine/Legends|Palpatine]] with [[Darth Plagueis/Legends|Darth Plagueis]], and many others. One of his favorite pieces was of [[Obi-Wan Kenobi/Legends|Obi-Wan Kenobi's]] [[Force ghost/Legends|Force ghost]] visiting [[Luke Skywalker/Legends|Luke Skywalker]] on [[Hoth/Legends|Hoth]].<ref name="Force-Cast">{{WebCite|url=http://www.theforce.net/latestnews/story/Microcast_In_The_Cantina_With_Chris_Tommy_110113.asp|text=Microcast: In The Cantina With Chris & Tommy|date=2007-11-13|archivedate=20160115154242}}</ref> Trevas again collaborated with Ryder Windham for ''[[Star Wars Blueprints: The Ultimate Collection]]'', which was released in August [[2008]]. In [[2009]], Trevas worked as one of the artists on ''[[The Essential Atlas]]'', alongside [[Ian Fullwood]], [[Modi]], and Chris Reiff.

Trevas worked on the book ''From a Certain Point of View'', providing illustrations for all of the short stories within, save for the story "[[Far Too Remote]]" by [[Jeffrey Brown]].
[[File:Mara-funeral.jpg|thumb|right|200px|The [[funeral of Mara Jade Skywalker]], an example of Trevas's detailed, full-color rendering style and attention to visual continuity for [[Star Wars Legends|Expanded Universe]] characters]]

==Style==
===Influences===
Both as a child and in his professional career, Trevas cites ''Star Wars'' conceptual artist [[Ralph McQuarrie]] as one of his major influences. McQuarrie's work inspired him to concentrate on illustrating environments, not just characters. Another important influence, particularly because of his mixing of pencil and paint, was poster artist [[Drew Struzan]]. More recently, Trevas has looked to the work of digital artists such as [[Ryan Church]] and Craig Mullens.<ref name="Star Wars Artist Series" />

===Technique===
The illustrative style of Chris Trevas has evolved over the years. While he used colored pencils during high school, he began to mix them with paint and eventually moved to using acrylic exclusively. Years later, Trevas switched to painting digitally in [[Wikipedia:Adobe Photoshop|Adobe Photoshop]] via a [[Wikipedia:Wacom|Wacom]] [[Wikipedia:Graphics tablet|drawing tablet]]. He also uses [[Wikipedia:Adobe Illustrator|Adobe Illustrator]] for line art drawings.<ref name="Star Wars Artist Series" />

*{{SWArchive|url=eu/explore/profile/f20041209/index.html|text=''Star Wars'' Artist Series: Chris Trevas}}
